name: ExtensionAdverseEvent_Identifier
title: R5: identifier
purpose:
This extension is part of the cross-version definitions generated to enable use of the
element `AdverseEvent.identifier` as defined in FHIR R5
in FHIR R4.

The source element is defined as:
`AdverseEvent.identifier` 0..* `Identifier`

Across FHIR versions, the element set has been mapped as:
*  R5: `AdverseEvent.identifier` 0..* `Identifier`
*  R4B: `AdverseEvent.identifier` 0..1 `Identifier`
*  R4: `AdverseEvent.identifier` 0..1 `Identifier`

Following are the generation technical comments:
Element `AdverseEvent.identifier` allows multiple values in the source, but is mapped to an element that does not allow multiple values in the target.
Element `AdverseEvent.identifier` is mapped to FHIR R4 element `AdverseEvent.identifier` as `SourceIsBroaderThanTarget`.

element.comment:
Element `AdverseEvent.identifier` allows multiple values in the source, but is mapped to an element that does not allow multiple values in the target.
Element `AdverseEvent.identifier` is mapped to FHIR R4 element `AdverseEvent.identifier` as `SourceIsBroaderThanTarget`.
This is a business identifier, not a resource identifier (see [discussion](https://hl7.org/fhir/resource.htm#identifiers)).  It is best practice for the identifier to only appear on a single resource instance, however business practices may occasionally dictate that multiple resource instances with the same identifier can exist - possibly even with different resource types.  For example, multiple Patient and a Person resource instance might share the same social insurance number.

element.definition: Business identifiers assigned to this adverse event by the performer or other systems which remain constant as the resource is updated and propagates from server to server.
element.comment: This is a business identifier, not a resource identifier (see [discussion](https://hl7.org/fhir/resource.htm#identifiers)).  It is best practice for the identifier to only appear on a single resource instance, however business practices may occasionally dictate that multiple resource instances with the same identifier can exist - possibly even with different resource types.
